41: A direct revelation: Talitha koum — little girl, I say to you, arise. The resurrection of Jairus's daughter is the first explicit raising-from-death in Mark, performed with a simple Aramaic command. The contrast with later resurrection narratives (the elaborate prayer of Elijah in 1 Kings 17:21, the stretching of Elisha over the boy in 2 Kings 4:34-35) is stark: Jesus does not pray, does not invoke God, does not perform a ritual — he speaks to the dead girl as to a sleeping child. The command-authority over death revealed here is the authority that will be exercised in his own resurrection and in the final resurrection.
1: A shadow of mission to the nations: Jesus crosses to Gentile Gerasene territory — beyond the Jewish side of the lake, beyond the boundaries of Israel's covenant. The movement into the "other side" (peran) recurs throughout Mark as a pattern of Christ going where the lost are, not waiting for them to come to him. The incarnation itself is this crossing: God into human territory, holiness into the place of uncleanness.
2: A revelation of God coming to the abandoned: a man from the tombs — the place of death, ritual uncleanness, and social exile — runs to meet Jesus. Christ's first encounter in Gentile territory is not with the respectable but with the most catastrophically isolated person imaginable. The direction of the gospel is always toward the tombs, the excluded, the unreachable (cf. Luke 19:10: "the Son of Man came to seek and save the lost").
3: A theme of human powerlessness before spiritual bondage: the man cannot be bound, no human strength can contain him. The catalog of failed chains and fetters establishes that what holds the man is beyond human remediation. Only the one stronger than the strong man (Mark 3:27) can enter the strong man's house and free his captives. Christ comes precisely to where human power ends.
4: A theme of the failed human remedies for spiritual bondage: the escalating futility — chained, broke chains, fettered, broke fetters, no one strong enough to subdue him — frames the entrance of the one who subdues all things (Philippians 3:21: Christ "will transform our lowly bodies so that they will be like his glorious body, by the power that enables him to bring everything under his control"). What no man could do, Christ does with a word.
5: A revelation of what spiritual bondage looks like: night and day, among the tombs, crying out, cutting himself — this is the condition of the one who belongs to the unclean powers. The image is extreme but representative of the condition of all who are under the dominion of death and sin (Ephesians 2:1-3: "dead in your transgressions and sins"). Christ enters this landscape not to observe it but to end it.
6: A revelation of Christ's lordship over the demonic: "he ran and fell on his knees in front of him" — the demon-controlled man performs the gesture of submission to Christ before a word is spoken. Even the forces of spiritual darkness bow before him. Philippians 2:10 ("at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth") is enacted here in microcosm: the knee of the demonized is the first to bow.
7: A direct revelation from demonic testimony: "Jesus, Son of the Most High God" — the demons recognize Jesus's identity before the disciples do. The title "Son of the Most High God" (huios tou theou tou hypsistou) uses the Gentile form of the divine name (the El Elyon of Melchizedek and the Gentile traditions, Genesis 14:18-22), suggesting recognition across the boundaries of Jewish messianic categories. The tormentor recognizes the one who has authority to torment the tormentors.
8: A theme of Christ's authority over impure spirits: "Come out of this man, you impure spirit!" — the same command as at Capernaum (Mark 1:25-26), now in Gentile territory. The scope of Christ's exorcistic authority knows no geographic boundary. Colossians 2:15 ("he disarmed the powers and authorities, he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross") identifies the cross as the definitive exorcism; these Galilean and Decapolis exorcisms are signs of what the cross accomplishes.
9: A revelation of Christ's authority over named demonic powers: "My name is Legion" — Christ compels the disclosure of identity. In the ancient world, knowing and naming a spirit gave power over it; here it is Christ who asks and the demons who must answer. The name Legion (a Roman military unit of ~6,000) also underscores the scale of the bondage. Christ's authority extends not just to individual spirits but to organized demonic assemblies (Ephesians 6:12: "the powers and authorities, the spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly realms").
10: A theme of demonic subjection to Christ's judgment: the demons beg "again and again not to send them out of the area" — the request implies they know they are under Christ's jurisdiction and cannot resist his decision. Their begging is itself a declaration of his authority. The cosmic powers that oppose God beg for terms from the one who has authority over them — an anticipation of the final judgment when all enemies are placed under Christ's feet (1 Corinthians 15:25).
11: A theme of providential setting: the nearby herd of pigs — a Gentile detail that would mark this as unclean territory for any Jewish reader — is the instrument through which the man's liberation becomes visible to the community. God uses what is present in the place he enters. The story's Gentile context (pigs, Decapolis) makes it a foreshadowing of the Gentile mission in which Christ's liberating power will operate.
12: A revelation that Christ's presence is inescapable judgment for the demonic: even their request to go into the pigs is a petition — they cannot act without his permission. The demons' request to enter unclean animals in unclean territory is the irony of powers that sought dominion being confined to the lowest place. Christ's consent to their request gives them what they asked for, which leads to their destruction — a pattern of divine permission that serves divine purpose.
13: A theme of liberation through apparent loss: the pigs rush into the lake; the local community loses an economic asset; the man gains his freedom. The cost of liberation is real — the pigs, the economic disruption — but the value of the person freed outweighs it (Luke 15:7: "there will be more rejoicing in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous persons who do not need to repent"). Christ consistently treats human restoration as worth economic and social disruption.
14: A theme of the world's response to Christ's liberation: the pig-herders run to report, the townspeople come to see. The news of a miraculous event draws witnesses. But what they find (Mark 5:15) frightens them rather than drawing them. The pattern anticipates Gethsemane and the trial: witnesses to extraordinary events about Christ respond with fear, flight, or hostility rather than faith.
15: A revelation of what Christ's salvation looks like: the formerly demonized man "sitting there, dressed and in his right mind" — three particulars of restoration. Sitting (not running and crying out), clothed (not naked and unrestrained), in his right mind (not controlled by alien powers). This is the image of salvation: the humanity that was lost to bondage, restored to its God-given dignity. The observers' fear in the face of this restoration is the world's characteristic response to grace it did not expect and cannot categorize.
16: A theme of witness to Christ's power: those who had seen it told what happened — to the man and to the pigs. The double witness (the human restoration, the dramatic sign of the pigs) is offered to the community. The community will reject it (Mark 5:17). The pattern of witnessed miracle followed by community rejection anticipates the rejection Jesus will face more broadly.
17: A theme of the world's rejection of Christ: "they began to plead with Jesus to leave their region" — having seen the miracle and heard the testimony, the Gerasene community asks Jesus to leave. The liberation of the man is less valuable to them than the disruption of the status quo. The same structure recurs throughout Mark: Nazareth (Mark 6:3), Jerusalem (Mark 14-15). The world consistently prefers its own disordered familiarity to the disruptive presence of the one who makes all things new.
18: A theme of the redeemed clinging to the Redeemer: the healed man "begged to go with him" — the natural response of one whose life has been restored. The desire to remain with Jesus is the proper movement of the saved soul toward its Savior. But Christ redirects this desire into mission (Mark 5:19), showing that the love for Christ is expressed through witness, not simply through accompaniment.
19: A revelation of Christ commissioning the first Gentile witness: "Go home to your own people and tell them how much the Lord has done for you, and how he has had mercy on you." The first person explicitly commissioned to testify about Jesus in Mark is a Gentile, in Gentile territory, before the formal sending of the Twelve (Mark 6:7). The Gentile mission is not an afterthought to Mark's Gospel; it begins here, commissioned by Jesus himself. "How much the Lord has done" — ho kyrios in the mouth of Jesus refers to God; the man will proclaim "how much Jesus had done" (Mark 5:20), equating the two.
20: A revelation of the Gentile mission as proclamation of Christ: the man "began to tell in the Decapolis how much Jesus had done for him." He substitutes "Jesus" for "the Lord" (Mark 5:19) — making the equation that the Christology of the NT will develop: what God does, Jesus does. The Decapolis proclamation anticipates the scope of the NT mission to Gentiles (Acts 1:8: "to the ends of the earth"). All were amazed — the Gentile world's response to Christ's power is available to it from the beginning.
21: A theme of Christ's continual accessibility: he crosses back to the Jewish side and immediately "a large crowd gathered around him." The one who had gone to the far Gentile shore returns to be available to the Jewish crowd — the movement between communities (Jewish/Gentile, religious leader/hemorrhaging outcast) is the pattern of his universal mission.
22: A revelation of Christ as the hope of every level of society: Jairus, a synagogue ruler — a person of religious authority, social standing — "fell at his feet." The posture of humility before Christ crosses social boundaries. The same posture as the Gerasene demoniac (Mark 5:6) and the hemorrhaging woman (Mark 5:33): all who come to Jesus in genuine need adopt the posture of dependence. Rank counts for nothing; need counts for everything.
23: A theme of death as the ultimate problem that only Christ can solve: "my little daughter is dying" — the father's earnest pleading (the same verb, parakalein, as in Mark 5:10 where the demons plead) expresses the absolute limit of paternal love and human power. Death is the one problem before which all human competence fails. The Gospel of Mark structures this encounter so that it will escalate from dying to dead before Christ acts — making the demonstration of his power over death unambiguous.
24: A theme of Christ going to the place of need: "Jesus went with him" — the uncomplicated willingness to accompany the desperate father. No hesitation, no negotiation, no preconditions. The divine willingness to go to the place of human need is the pattern of the incarnation itself: the Word came to where we are (John 1:14).
25: A theme of persistent suffering that finds its end only in Christ: twelve years of hemorrhaging — a period of chronic, unresolvable affliction. The woman's condition has been continuous, exhausting, and isolating (ritual uncleanness under Leviticus 15:25-27 cut her off from communal worship and social contact). The length of suffering intensifies the significance of the healing. Christ does not require that suffering be brief before he addresses it.
26: A theme of the exhaustion of human remedies: "she had suffered a great deal under the care of many doctors and had spent all she had, yet instead of getting better she grew worse." The catalog of failed human attempts — money spent, doctors consulted, condition worsened — frames Christ as the one resource that succeeds where all others fail. This is not anti-medicine but narrative theology: when all human resources are exhausted, Christ remains.
27: A theme of faith reaching toward Christ from behind, anonymously: she came "behind him" and touched his cloak. The anonymous, unacknowledged approach — from the back of the crowd, touching clothing rather than the person — is the faith of those who dare not present themselves directly. Christ meets this faith too, and will not let it remain anonymous (Mark 5:32). No one who touches Christ in genuine faith goes unrecognized by him.
28: A theme of the faith that reaches toward Christ: "if I just touch his clothes, I will be healed." The reasoning is not superstitious (as if the garment contained magic) but typological: she has heard what Jesus does and believes that contact with him — even indirect, even marginal — is sufficient. This is the structure of all saving faith: confidence that Christ himself, touched by faith, is adequate to the need.
29: A revelation of the immediate efficacy of faith-contact with Christ: "immediately her bleeding stopped and she felt in her body that she was freed from her suffering." The healing is total, immediate, and physically felt. The word "freed" (iaomai) — healed, made whole — is the vocabulary of salvation and restoration. Faith that reaches Christ always receives what it reaches for.
30: A revelation of Christ as the source of healing power: "power had gone out from him" (dynamis ex autou exelthyousa) — Christ's healing is not performance but emanation; something went out from him. The vocabulary suggests that Christ is the reservoir from which healing flows, not merely an agent through whom it passes. This is consistent with Colossians 1:19 ("God was pleased to have all his fullness dwell in him") and John 1:16 ("from his fullness we have all received").
31: A theme of the disciples' failure to perceive what Christ perceives: "You see the people crowding against you, and yet you can ask, 'Who touched me?'" — the disciples see crowd pressure where Jesus perceives faith. The difference is not physical but spiritual: Jesus distinguishes the touch of faith from accidental contact. The disciples will repeatedly miss what Jesus sees; faith is the instrument of perception that they do not yet fully possess.
32: A revelation of Christ's personal attention to the one who touched him in faith: "he kept looking around to see who had done it." The Son of God, surrounded by thousands, searches for one person — the one who touched him in faith. No one who comes to Christ in genuine faith becomes one of the anonymous crowd to him. He knows his own (John 10:14), and seeks out each one.
33: A theme of the movement from anonymous faith to confessed faith: the woman "knowing what had happened to her, came and fell at his feet and, trembling with fear, told him the whole truth." She had touched him anonymously; now she falls at his feet and confesses. The pattern is the same in every genuine encounter with Christ: anonymous reaching becomes named, confessed discipleship. The one who meets Christ in faith cannot remain hidden.
34: A direct revelation of Christ granting peace and wholeness: "Daughter, your faith has healed you. Go in peace and be freed from your suffering." The address "Daughter" is the familial declaration — she is adopted into the family of the one who heals her. "Go in peace" (hypage eis eirēnēn) is the priestly blessing formula (cf. Judges 18:6; 1 Samuel 1:17), here given by the Great High Priest. "Be freed from your suffering" — the perfect, complete, future-guaranteed liberation is Christ's gift to the one who came in faith.
35: A revelation of Christ as the answer to death, not just illness: while Jesus is still speaking about the healed woman, news arrives that Jairus's daughter has died. The escalation from dying to dead is the story's Christological core: Christ is not limited to healing the sick; his authority extends over death itself. The timing — "while Jesus was still speaking" — underscores that the power at work in the woman's healing and the power that will raise Jairus's daughter are the same power.
36: A direct revelation of Christ calling for faith against death: "Don't be afraid; just believe." The command is addressed to Jairus, whose circumstances have just become worst-case. The word for "believe" (pisteue) — present imperative — means "keep on believing"; the faith already present must persist through the news of death. Christ's call to faith against death is the call of the gospel itself: believe the one who says "I am the resurrection" (John 11:25) even when death has arrived.
37: A theme of Christ preparing selected witnesses for a resurrection revelation: Peter, James, and John — the inner three — are taken with Jesus to the house of death. They will be the witnesses to three defining Christological events: the resurrection of Jairus's daughter (here), the transfiguration (Mark 9:2), and Gethsemane (Mark 14:33). The narrowing of witness is not exclusion but preparation: these men will be foundational witnesses for what the church will believe about Jesus.
38: A theme of Christ entering the house of death: "Jesus saw a commotion, with people crying and wailing loudly" — professional mourners and weeping family have gathered because they believe the girl is dead. Jesus's entrance into this house of mourning is the movement of the incarnation in miniature: the Lord of life entering the place where death has established itself. He goes where death has already claimed its victim.
39: A revelation of Christ transforming the category of death: "The child is not dead but asleep" — the language anticipates the NT transformation of death for those who are in Christ. 1 Thessalonians 4:14 ("those who have fallen asleep in him") uses the same sleep-language because of the resurrection that Christ demonstrates here. Death is not annihilated but reclassified: for those in Christ, it becomes the sleep from which resurrection awakens them. The mourners laugh because they do not know the resurrection; the gospel calls people to believe before they see it.
40: A theme of the world's contempt for resurrection hope: "they laughed at him" — the professional mourners (and presumably others) mock Jesus's reclassification of death as sleep. The laughter is the world's characteristic response to resurrection: 1 Corinthians 15:12 records the same skepticism ("how can some of you say there is no resurrection of the dead?"). Christ does not engage the mockery but puts it out of the room and proceeds to demonstrate the truth of what he has declared.
42: A direct type of the resurrection: "immediately the girl stood up and began to walk around." Her resurrection is a direct anticipation of the general resurrection and a type of Christ's own: the one who raises the dead will himself be raised (1 Corinthians 15:20: "Christ has indeed been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep"). Jairus's daughter and Lazarus (John 11) and the widow's son (Luke 7:11-17) are preliminary signs of the resurrection that Christ will accomplish for all who are in him.
43: A theme of the Messianic Secret at the resurrection miracle: "give strict orders not to let anyone know about this" — the most astonishing miracle so far (a raised-from-the-dead child, multiple witnesses, a synagogue ruler as recipient) immediately placed under silence. The Secret pattern reaches its most paradoxical moment here: how does one conceal a raised child? The command cannot succeed practically, but it establishes that Jesus defines the terms of the messianic revelation. The resurrection will be proclaimed — but on Christ's terms, not the crowd's.
